title: Charmed Life - Chapter 16 (Fragment 4)
text:
“You poor darlings! Another time you get unhappy, you must come and tell me at once ! And what a thing too! I was so afraid you’d got into real trouble, and then Julia told me it was her. I’m extremely vexed with her. A girl did that to me once and I know how miserable it made me. Now, please, please come back. I’ve got a surprise waiting for you at the Castle.”

There was nothing Cat and Janet could do but climb into the back of the car and be driven back to the Castle. They were miserable. Cat’s misery was increased by the fact that he began to feel sick from the moment Millie started to bump the car backwards down the lane to a gate where she could turn it. The smell of blackberry coming from Janet’s squashy hat made him feel worse.

Millie, Roger, and Julia were very relieved to have found them. They chattered joyfully the whole way. Through his sickness, Cat got the impression that, although none of them said so, what they were particularly glad about was to have found Janet and Cat before Chrestomanci came to hear they were gone. This did not make either Cat or Janet feel any better.

In five minutes, the car had whispered up the avenue and stopped at the main door of the Castle. The butler opened it for them just, Cat thought sadly, as Gwendolen would have wished. The butler, furthermore, ceremoniously took Janet’s leaking hat away from her. “I’ll see that these get to Cook,” he said.

Millie told Janet that her dress would just pass muster and hurried them to what was called the Little Drawing Room. “Which means, of course, that it’s a mere seventy feet square,” she said. “Go in. Tea will be there for you.”

They went in. In the middle of the big, square room, a wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair. She jumped around when the door opened.

Cat forgot he felt sick. “Mrs. Sharp!” he shouted, and ran to hug her.

Mrs. Sharp was overjoyed, in spite of her nervousness. “It’s my Cat, then! Here, stand back, let me look at you, and you too, Gwendolen, love. My word, you do wear fine clothes to go playing about in! You’re fatter, Cat. And Gwendolen, you’ve gone thin. I can understand that, dear, believe me! And would you just look at the tea they’ve brought for the three of us!”

It was a marvelous tea, even better than the tea on the lawn. Mrs. Sharp, in her old greedy way, settled down to eat as much as she could, and to gossip hard. “Yes, we came up on the train yesterday, Mr. Nostrum and me. After I got your postcard, Cat, I couldn’t rest till I’d had a look at you both, and seeing as how my contacts and other things have been paying nicely, I felt I owed it to myself. They treated me like royalty when I turned up here at the door too. I can’t fault them. But I wish I cared for it in this Castle. Tell me, Gwendolen, love, does it get you like it gets me?”

“How does it get you?” Janet asked cautiously.

“I’m nerves all over,” said Mrs. Sharp. “I feel weak and jumpy as a kitten—and that reminds me, Cat, but I’ll tell you later. It’s so quiet here. I kept trying to think what it was before you came—and you were a long time, my loves—and at last it came to me. It’s an enchantment, that’s what it is, a terrible strong one too, against us witches. I said, ‘This Castle does not love witches, that’s what it is!’ and I felt for you, Gwendolen. Make him send you to school away somewhere. You’d be happier.”

She chattered on. She was delighted to see them both, and she kept giving Cat particularly proud and affectionate looks. Cat thought she had convinced herself she had brought him up from a baby. After all, she had known him since he was born.

vocabulary.definition: Showing fondness or tenderness.
vocabulary.example: She kept giving Cat particularly proud and affectionate looks.

vocabulary.definition: In a formal and dignified manner showing observance of ceremony.
vocabulary.example: The butler ceremoniously took Janet’s leaking hat away from her.

vocabulary.definition: To continue talking in an informal way.
vocabulary.example: She chattered on. She was delighted to see them both.

vocabulary.definition: To talk quickly or incessantly about trivial matters.
vocabulary.example: They chattered joyfully the whole way.

vocabulary.definition: People one knows who can be helpful professionally or socially.
vocabulary.example: My contacts and other things have been paying nicely.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing great pleasure.
vocabulary.example: She was delighted to see them both.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell or charm; a feeling of great delight or attraction.
vocabulary.example: It’s an enchantment, that’s what it is, a terrible strong one too, against us witches.

vocabulary.definition: Easily broken or damaged.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously.

vocabulary.definition: Covered thinly with gold leaf or gold paint.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair.

vocabulary.definition: Casual or unconstrained conversation or reports about other people, typically involving details that are not confirmed as true.
vocabulary.example: She settled down to eat as much as she could, and to gossip hard.

vocabulary.definition: Having an intense and selfish desire for something, especially wealth or food.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp, in her old greedy way, settled down to eat as much as she could.

vocabulary.definition: To move or act with haste; to be quick.
vocabulary.example: Millie told Janet that her dress would just pass muster and hurried them to the Little Drawing Room.

vocabulary.definition: An idea, feeling, or opinion about something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Cat got the impression that they were particularly glad about finding Janet and Cat before Chrestomanci came to hear they were gone.

vocabulary.definition: To become a member or to be part of something.
vocabulary.example: Tea will be there for you.

vocabulary.definition: Causing great wonder; extraordinary.
vocabulary.example: It was a marvelous tea, even better than the tea on the lawn.

vocabulary.definition: A state of great suffering and unhappiness.
vocabulary.example: They were miserable.

vocabulary.definition: A gathering or inspection, or the quality to be acceptable or sufficient.
vocabulary.example: Millie told Janet that her dress would just pass muster and hurried them to the Little Drawing Room.

vocabulary.definition: The quality or state of being anxious or worried about something.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely happy or pleased.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp was overjoyed, in spite of her nervousness.

vocabulary.definition: A card for sending a message by mail without an envelope, typically having a picture on one side.
vocabulary.example: After I got your postcard, Cat, I couldn’t rest till I’d had a look at you both.

vocabulary.definition: No longer feeling distressed or anxious; reassured.
vocabulary.example: Millie, Roger, and Julia were very relieved to have found them.

vocabulary.definition: Members of a royal family; also a term used metaphorically to mean treated with great respect or honour.
vocabulary.example: They treated me like royalty when I turned up here at the door too.

vocabulary.definition: To arrive or appear somewhere, often unexpectedly.
vocabulary.example: They treated me like royalty when I turned up here at the door too.

vocabulary.definition: Annoyed, frustrated, or worried.
vocabulary.example: I’m extremely vexed with her.

vocabulary.definition: Fine, feathery, or slight in form or appearance.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair.
